claim: An 11,022-token excerpt of a medieval Latin herbal-encyclopedic text shows the same paragraph-structure signature as Hand A — under 10% header recurrence, paragraph-medial vocabulary drawn from a shared descriptive pool, and anti-alternating HIGH/LOW runs — confirming that the herbal-encyclopedic framework is the correct interpretation for Hand A. If the benchmark text instead shows >=30% header recurrence (classical pharmaceutical recipe pattern) or shows natural alternation at adjacency, the herbal-encyclopedic reframe from H-BV-RECIPE-STRUCTURE-01 is refuted.
evidence_for: H-BV-RECIPE-STRUCTURE-01 MARGINAL (2/3) pattern — strong vocabulary disjunction and recipe-verb properties but low header recurrence — matches the profile of medieval herbal encyclopedias where each plant has a unique entry

H-BV-HIGH-LOW-STRUCTURE-01 anti-alternation at cross-class 46.9% is consistent with run-clustering of descriptive register (plant name region + property region) rather than interleaved function/content

Medieval herbal-encyclopedic texts (Circa Instans c.1150, Macer Floridus 11th c.) have the predicted structure: alphabetically-arranged single-plant entries with unique headers and heavily-reused descriptive vocabulary (degrees, qualities, uses, preparation verbs)
evidence_against: Circa Instans is not available in clean digital plain text at time of pre-registration (the Internet Archive djvu OCR is badly corrupted); this test must use Macer Floridus (same period, same genre) as primary benchmark, which is VERSE not prose — meter constraints may affect word length and vocabulary distribution in ways that distort the comparison
The H-BV-RECIPE-STRUCTURE-01 header-recurrence criterion (10%) may be wrong in either direction — a benchmark test is precisely what determines the correct threshold

A Latin herbal-encyclopedic text under this pipeline may show paragraph detection artifacts because paragraph boundaries in medieval Latin texts are explicit (chapter headers, capital letters) rather than inferred from gallows markers as in Voynich
test:
LOCKED PROCEDURE.

  CORPUS (frozen at pre-registration):
    Macer Floridus, De viribus herbarum, Choulant edition (Leipzig 1832),
    as fetched to `raw/corpus/reference-corpora/macer_floridus_raw.txt` from
    archive.org/details/deviribusherbaru00mace (djvu text).
    Rationale: Circa Instans itself is not available in clean digital
    plain-text form; Macer Floridus is the closest period-matched,
    genre-matched (single-plant encyclopedic entries), publicly-available
    Latin herbal. Verse form is the known limitation; acknowledged.

  CLEANING (locked):
    - Discard all lines before the first chapter heading (line 2278
      'IIl. ABSINTHIUM.') and after the Macer section end (first line
      matching '^CONFECTBUCH|^Confectbuch').
    - Discard lines that: (a) contain any Greek character (Unicode ranges
      U+0370..U+03FF or U+1F00..U+1FFF), (b) start with a digit followed
      by space, (c) match the pattern for chapter heading (lines like
      'IVL. PLANT.' — roman numeral + period + plant in caps), (d) contain
      fewer than 3 Latin-word tokens, (e) contain ONLY footnote-apparatus
      markers (Greek letters outside the Greek Unicode range are rare
      but can be approximated by 3-or-more single-char tokens).
    - Lowercase, strip punctuation, keep only ASCII letters and spaces.
    - Truncate to first 11,022 word tokens.

  PARAGRAPH DETECTION (adapted for Macer):
    Since Macer's chapter headings are the natural paragraph boundaries,
    a paragraph in this corpus = the content between two chapter heading
    lines (roman-numeral + plant-name uppercase). Unlike Hand A where
    paragraphs start at gallows markers, Macer's paragraphs start at
    chapter headings, which are clearly delimited in the source text.

  PIPELINE (identical to H-BV-RECIPE-STRUCTURE-01):
    Compute:
      (1) HIGH/LOW split at rank where cumulative tokens reach 50%
          (R_Macer may differ from R_HandA=146).
      (2) Vocabulary disjunction (|INITIAL \ MIDDLE| / |INITIAL|).
      (3) Top-20 MIDDLE-position token properties (inflection +
          cross-chapter coverage); inflection via consonant-skeleton
          stemming (strip Latin vowels), cross-chapter via appearance
          in >=30% of chapters.
      (4) Header recurrence rate: fraction of INITIAL types appearing
          at paragraph-initial in >=3 paragraphs.
      (5) Cross-class adjacency rate HL+LH.

  REPORT all five measures side-by-side against the Hand A values from
  H-BV-HIGH-LOW-STRUCTURE-01 and H-BV-RECIPE-STRUCTURE-01.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED herbal-encyclopedic if Macer Floridus simultaneously shows: (a) header recurrence < 10% (matches Hand A 4.6%), AND (b) HIGH/LOW structural split with vocabulary disjunction >=0.70 AND top-20 medial verb reuse >=15/20, AND (c) cross-class adjacency rate < 0.52 (anti-alternating). REFUTED if Macer shows header recurrence >= 30% (pharmaceutical recipe pattern where ingredients recur) OR cross-class adjacency >=0.55 (natural-language alternation — would mean Hand A's anti-alternation is not a herbal feature). MARGINAL on any intermediate outcome, with follow-up required.
tests_remaining: Execute Macer Floridus benchmark comparison in separate commit after lock
reasoning: H-BV-RECIPE-STRUCTURE-01 produced a MARGINAL 2/3 verdict that substantively resolved to herbal-encyclopedic rather than classical recipe. The next locked test needs to benchmark against an actual medieval herbal-encyclopedic text. Circa Instans itself is the canonical reference but no clean digital plain text exists; Macer Floridus is the closest period-matched, genre-matched public-domain source. If Macer reproduces Hand A's three signature features (low header recurrence, vocabulary disjunction, anti-alternation), the reframe is confirmed; if Macer differs on any of the three by the specified margin, the reframe needs revision.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-16 user-directed benchmark test following RECIPE-STRUCTURE-01 MARGINAL result
